entry.title: Re: Pushing data from datasphere to SFTP through replicat...
entry.content:
Hi,

This is the expected behavior of Replication Flows and is currently not configurable.

  1. Can the Initial folder be avoided?
    No. The Initial folder is automatically created for initial loads and cannot be removed or bypassed.
  2. Can the output file have a static name?
    No. The file name is system-generated and there is currently no option to define a custom/static name.

For more details, please refer to the SAP Help documentation for Replication Flows with SFTP targets

If this functionality is important for your use case, we recommend submitting an enhancement request via https://influence.sap.com/go/datasphere

Thanks,

Farah

entry.title: Re: ewm stock counting for batch with 0 stock
entry.content:
Click on create icon in /SCWM/PI_CREATE.

Enter the Material code, batch, Stock type, Disposal, Owner, Bin Detail and save it.. Upon saving it would create a Physical Inventory document for which you can perform the count in the subsequent step.

entry.title: Re: Function YTD() not working in Datasphere
entry.content:
Hello,

 

The YTD() function only works when the date dimension is correctly configured and used in the restricted measure.

If the YTD value is not shown in the data preview, please verify:

  • The date dimension is assigned with the correct time semantics.
  • The model contains data for the selected time period.
  • The YTD() function is applied to the correct date dimension.

 

Thanks,

Farah

entry.title: Using Refresh Token to Consume SAC Multi-Actions Public API
entry.content:
Introduction

 

SAC Multi-Actions let you chain planning operations — such as data action,  data locking, and version management — into a single execution sequence. While these are typically triggered from the SAC UI or scheduler, many enterprise scenarios demand programmatic invocation from external systems: as part of an SAP Integration Suite flow, or from a custom microservice running on SAP BTP.
 
The SAC Multi-Actions Public API enables this, but with an important constraint: it does not support OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials. All calls must run under a business user context. There are two common patterns to achieve non-interactive authentication while preserving user identity: the OAuth 2.0 refresh token flow (covered in detail in this post), and the OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion flow . This blog walks through how to use refresh tokens to consume the SAC Multi-Actions Public API end-to-end.

Understanding the Authentication Challenge

Why Not Client Credentials?

The SAC Multi-Actions API requires a business user context because planning operations involve data security, audit trails, and user-specific permissions. A service principal (client credentials) cannot own planning data or appear in audit logs. Therefore, every API call must carry a user identity.

The Role of Refresh Token

In OAuth 2.0, the authorization server issues two types of tokens: access token and refresh token. While access token grant short-lived access to protected resources, refresh token are used to acquire a new access token when the current one expires — without requiring the user to re-authenticate interactively.
 
For automated integrations, this means:
  • You perform interactive authentication once to obtain the initial access token and refresh token
  • Your application stores the refresh token securely
  • When the access token expires, your application uses the refresh token to get a new access token automatically
  • The integration continues running without human intervention
     

OAuth2 Refresh Token Flow

1. Enable External API Access on Multi-Actions

In SAC, open your Multi-Actions designer, click the settings icon, and check Allow External API Access. Save the changes and note down the Starter API URL — this is your Multi-Actions execution endpoint.
 

2. Create an OAuth Client in SAC

Navigate to System > Administration > App Integration and create a new OAuth client with these settings:
  • Purpose: Interactive Usage
  • Authorization Grant: Authorization Code
  • Redirect URI: https://oauth.usebruno.com/callback which is required by Bruno
  • Token Lifetime: Access tokens are short-lived, typically valid for 1 hour
  • Refresh Token Lifetime: Refresh tokens are valid for up to 180 days, and are used to obtain new access tokens       

After saving, copy the Client ID and Client Secret (the secret is only shown once). Also note the Token URL and Authorization URL displayed on the same page.

3. Configure OAuth 2.0 in Bruno

Go to the "Auth" tab of your request collection in Bruno, and configure a new OAuth 2.0 token with the following parameters:
  • Grant Type: Authorization Code
  • Callback URL: https://oauth.usebruno.com/callback 
  • Auth URL: The Authorization URL copied from SAC
  • Access Token URL: The Token URL copied from SAC
  • Client ID: Your OAuth client ID
  • Client Secret: Your OAuth client secret        

4. Obtain the Initial Refresh Token

After configuration is done, click on "Get Access Token" at the bottom of  Bruno Auth tab.  After logging on SAC in the pop-up window, both an access_token and a refresh_token should be fetched. The step is to perform the standard Authorization Code flow, and this is a one-time interactive step. 

Important: Securely store the refresh_token — this is your long-lived credential. Never hardcode it in your application or commit it to version control.

 

5. Use Refresh Token to Get a New Access Token

When the access token expires (or proactively before expiry in production implementations), call the token endpoint with grant_type=refresh_token, along with your stored refresh token. The request uses Basic authentication with your OAuth Client ID and Client Secret.

6: Fetch CSRF Token

The access token can be used to access Multi-Actions public APIs. In addition, SAC protects write operations with CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) tokens to prevent unauthorized cross-origin requests. Before sending HTTP POST requests (used for triggering Multi-Actions), you must first fetch a valid CSRF token.
 
Send a GET request to {your-tenant-url}/api/v1/csrf, including the header x-csrf-token: fetch. The CSRF token is returned in the x-csrf-token response header.
 
The CSRF token is bound to the current user session and can be reused for the lifetime of the access token. GET requests do not require a CSRF token.
  • Authorization:  Bearer <valid access token>        

 
7: Trigger Multi-Actions Execution
Use the valid access token and CSRF token in the following POST request to execute a Multi-Actions.
  • Authorization:  Bearer <valid access token>
  • Content-Type:  application/json
  • x-csrf-token:  <valid  csrf token> 

8: Get Multi-Actions Execution Result

Use the query status API to get the status of Multi-Actions execution result.

  • Authorization:  Bearer <valid access token>        

Complete Node.js Example

Below is a complete Node.js + Express example that implements the full refresh token flow, automatic CSRF token fetching, and proactive token refresh. The example uses server-side sessions for demonstration purposes; for production use, replace the in-memory session with a persistent store and a dedicated secrets manager.

Environment Configuration

Create a .env file in your project root with the following variables:

SAC_CLIENT_ID=your-oauth-client-id
SAC_CLIENT_SECRET=your-oauth-client-secret
SAC_BASE_URL=https://your-tenant-url
SAC_TOKEN_URL=https://your-tenant-token-url
SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL=https://your-tenant-authorize-url
REDIRECT_URI=http://localhost:3000/callback
PORT=3000
SESSION_SECRET=your-secure-session-secret

Full Source Code

require('dotenv').config();
const express = require('express');
const session = require('express-session');
const axios = require('axios');
const crypto = require('crypto');
const path = require('path');

const app = express();
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;

// SAC OAuth 2.0 credentials
const SAC_CLIENT_ID     = process.env.SAC_CLIENT_ID;
const SAC_CLIENT_SECRET = process.env.SAC_CLIENT_SECRET;
const SAC_BASE_URL      = process.env.SAC_BASE_URL;
const SAC_TOKEN_URL     = process.env.SAC_TOKEN_URL;
const SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL = process.env.SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL;
const REDIRECT_URI      = process.env.REDIRECT_URI || `http://localhost:${PORT}/callback`;

// Refresh 60 seconds before expiry to avoid mid-request failures
const TOKEN_REFRESH_BUFFER = 60;

app.use(express.json());
app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public')));
app.use(session({
  secret: process.env.SESSION_SECRET || 'change-me-in-production',
  resave: false,
  saveUninitialized: false,
  cookie: { secure: false, maxAge: 60 * 60 * 1000 }
}));

// ── Helper: Generate Basic Auth header ───────────────────────────────────────
function getBasicAuth() {
  return Buffer.from(`${SAC_CLIENT_ID}:${SAC_CLIENT_SECRET}`).toString('base64');
}

// ── Helper: Check if access token needs refresh ──────────────────────────────
function tokenNeedsRefresh(req) {
  if (!req.session.accessToken || !req.session.tokenExpiresAt) return true;
  return Date.now() >= req.session.tokenExpiresAt - TOKEN_REFRESH_BUFFER * 1000;
}

// ── Helper: Fetch CSRF token from SAC ────────────────────────────────────────
async function fetchCsrfToken(accessToken) {
  const response = await axios.get(`${SAC_BASE_URL}/api/v1/csrf`, {
    headers: {
      Authorization: `Bearer ${accessToken}`,
      'x-csrf-token': 'fetch'
    }
  });
  return response.headers['x-csrf-token'];
}

// ── Step 1: Redirect user to SAC authorization page ─────────────────────────
app.get('/auth/sac', (req, res) => {
  if (!SAC_CLIENT_ID || !SAC_BASE_URL) {
    return res.status(500).json({ error: 'SAC_CLIENT_ID or SAC_BASE_URL not configured' });
  }

  const state = crypto.randomBytes(16).toString('hex');
  req.session.oauthState = state;

  const params = new URLSearchParams({
    response_type: 'code',
    client_id: SAC_CLIENT_ID,
    redirect_uri: REDIRECT_URI,
    state
  });

  res.redirect(`${SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL}?${params}`);
});

// ── Step 2: SAC redirects back with authorization code ──────────────────────
app.get('/callback', async (req, res) => {
  const { code, state, error, error_description } = req.query;

  if (error) {
    return res.redirect(`/?error=${encodeURIComponent(error_description || error)}`);
  }

  if (!state || state !== req.session.oauthState) {
    return res.redirect('/?error=invalid_state');
  }
  delete req.session.oauthState;

  if (!code) {
    return res.redirect('/?error=no_code');
  }

  try {
    // Exchange authorization code for access + refresh tokens
    const tokenResponse = await axios.post(
      SAC_TOKEN_URL,
      new URLSearchParams({
        grant_type: 'authorization_code',
        code,
        redirect_uri: REDIRECT_URI
      }).toString(),
      {
        headers: {
          'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded',
          Authorization: `Basic ${getBasicAuth()}`
        }
      }
    );

    const { access_token, refresh_token, token_type, expires_in, scope } = tokenResponse.data;

    req.session.accessToken     = access_token;
    req.session.refreshToken    = refresh_token || null;
    req.session.tokenType       = token_type;
    req.session.tokenExpiresAt  = Date.now() + expires_in * 1000;
    req.session.scope           = scope;

    res.redirect('/?success=true');
  } catch (err) {
    const detail = err.response?.data?.error_description || err.response?.data?.error || err.message;
    console.error('Token exchange error:', detail);
    res.redirect(`/?error=${encodeURIComponent(detail)}`);
  }
});

// ── Helper: Refresh access token ─────────────────────────────────────────────
async function refreshAccessToken(req) {
  const rt = req.session.refreshToken;
  if (!rt) throw new Error('No refresh token available');

  try {
    const tokenResponse = await axios.post(
      SAC_TOKEN_URL,
      new URLSearchParams({
        grant_type:    'refresh_token',
        refresh_token: rt
      }).toString(),
      {
        headers: {
          'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded',
          Authorization:  `Basic ${getBasicAuth()}`
        }
      }
    );

    const { access_token, refresh_token, expires_in, scope } = tokenResponse.data;

    req.session.accessToken    = access_token;
    req.session.tokenExpiresAt = Date.now() + expires_in * 1000;
    req.session.scope          = scope;

    // Handle token rotation: update stored refresh token if a new one is issued
    if (refresh_token) {
      req.session.refreshToken = refresh_token;
    }

    return access_token;
  } catch (err) {
    const detail = err.response?.data?.error_description || err.response?.data?.error || err.message;
    console.error('Refresh token exchange error:', detail);
    throw new Error(`Token refresh failed: ${detail}`);
  }
}

// ── Middleware: Ensure valid access token before API calls ───────────────────
async function requireValidToken(req, res, next) {
  try {
    if (tokenNeedsRefresh(req)) {
      await refreshAccessToken(req);
    }
    next();
  } catch (err) {
    res.status(401).json({ error: err.message });
  }
}

// ── API: Get current token status ───────────────────────────────────────────
app.get('/api/token', (req, res) => {
  if (!req.session.accessToken) {
    return res.status(401).json({ authenticated: false });
  }
  const token = req.session.accessToken;
  res.json({
    authenticated: true,
    token_type:    req.session.tokenType,
    expires_in:    Math.max(0, Math.floor((req.session.tokenExpiresAt - Date.now()) / 1000)),
    scope:         req.session.scope,
    token_preview: `${token.slice(0, 6)}${'*'.repeat(6)}${token.slice(-6)}`,
    token_length:  token.length,
    has_refresh_token: !!req.session.refreshToken
  });
});

// ── API: Manually trigger token refresh ─────────────────────────────────────
app.post('/api/refresh', requireValidToken, async (req, res) => {
  try {
    await refreshAccessToken(req);
    const token = req.session.accessToken;
    const newRt = req.session.refreshToken;
    res.json({
      success: true,
      token_preview: `${token.slice(0, 6)}${'*'.repeat(6)}${token.slice(-6)}`,
      token_length:  token.length,
      expires_in:    Math.floor((req.session.tokenExpiresAt - Date.now()) / 1000),
      refresh_token_rotated: !!req.session.refreshToken,
      new_refresh_token_preview: `${newRt.slice(0, 6)}${'*'.repeat(6)}${newRt.slice(-6)}`
    });
  } catch (err) {
    res.status(500).json({ error: err.message });
  }
});

// ── API: Trigger multi-action execution ─────────────────────────────────────
app.post('/api/multiactions/:multiActionsId/executions', requireValidToken, async (req, res) => {
  try {
    // Automatically fetch CSRF token before POST request
    const csrfToken = await fetchCsrfToken(req.session.accessToken);

    const response = await axios.post(
      `${SAC_BASE_URL}/api/v1/multiActions/${encodeURIComponent(req.params.multiActionsId)}/executions/`,
      req.body,
      {
        headers: {
          Authorization: `Bearer ${req.session.accessToken}`,
          'Content-Type': 'application/json',
          'x-csrf-token': csrfToken,
          Accept: 'application/json'
        }
      }
    );
    res.status(response.status).json(response.data);
  } catch (err) {
    const status = err.response?.status || 500;
    const errorDetail = err.response?.data?.messages?.[0]?.message || err.response?.data || err.message;
    res.status(status).json({ error: errorDetail });
  }
});

// ── API: Get multi-action execution status ──────────────────────────────────
app.get('/api/multiactions/:multiActionsId/executions/:executionId', requireValidToken, async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const response = await axios.get(
      `${SAC_BASE_URL}/api/v1/multiActions/${encodeURIComponent(req.params.multiActionsId)}/executions/${encodeURIComponent(req.params.executionId)}`,
      {
        headers: {
          Authorization: `Bearer ${req.session.accessToken}`,
          Accept: 'application/json'
        }
      }
    );
    res.json(response.data);
  } catch (err) {
    const status = err.response?.status || 500;
    const errorDetail = err.response?.data?.messages?.[0]?.message || err.response?.data || err.message;
    res.status(status).json({ error: errorDetail });
  }
});

// ── API: Logout and clear session ───────────────────────────────────────────
app.post('/api/logout', (req, res) => {
  req.session.destroy(() => res.json({ success: true }));
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server running at http://localhost:${PORT}`);
  const missing = ['SAC_CLIENT_ID', 'SAC_CLIENT_SECRET', 'SAC_BASE_URL', 'SAC_TOKEN_URL']
    .filter(k => !process.env[k]);
  if (missing.length) {
    console.warn(`WARNING: Missing environment variables: ${missing.join(', ')}. Copy .env.example to .env.`);
  }
});

Conclusion

Using refresh token to consume the SAP Analytics Cloud Multi-Actions Public API provides a robust, automated approach for integrating SAC planning operations into your enterprise workflows. By separating the initial user authentication from ongoing API access, refresh token enable long-running integrations without compromising security or requiring interactive logins.
 
The key to success lies in implementing proper token lifecycle management: secure storage of credentials, proactive refresh before access token expiry, correct handling of token rotation, and graceful degradation when the refresh token itself expires.
 
Whether you're triggering Multi-Actions orchestrating planning cycles from SAP Integration Suite, or building custom planning applications on BTP, the refresh token flow ensures your SAC integrations remain authenticated and operational around the clock.

entry.title: Purchasing info record not updated after creation of PO
entry.content:
I created purchase order environment using a purchasing info record and changed the price in the purchase order. I selected the Info Update indicator.

Afterwards, I executed the job Determine and Update the Price of Purchasing Info Record. The job does not show any results.

I can see that the purchase order number has been added as the reference document in the purchasing info record. However, when I create a new purchase order using the same purchasing info record, the old price is still proposed.

Could you please confirm whether this is the expected system behavior? If not, could you advise which prerequisites or configuration settings are required for the updated price to be used in subsequent purchase orders?

entry.title: Re: trying to build a career in sap supply chain manageme...
entry.content:
Hi @harini_karthikeyanf0bfd5 

Welcome to the SAP Learning Group, nice to meet you

If you are new to SAP and interested in building a career in Supply Chain Management (SCM), I would recommend starting with a structured learning path rather than jumping directly into certification preparation. 

A good approach might be:

  1. Visit our Supply Chain Management learn by product site  

    • There you find learning materials to learn how to build connected, risk-resilient, and sustainable supply chains, understand core business processes such as procurement, inventory management, production planning, warehousing, and logistics.
  2. Browse the supply chain management capabilities
    • Explore the learning paths, they provide a structured introduction and help you understand the various SCM solutions available in SAP.

  3. Gain hands-on experience

    • If possible, use SAP practice systems and exercises to become familiar with the applications and processes. Practical experience is extremely valuable when starting an SAP career.

  4. Connect with other learners

    • Consider joining the relevant SAP Learning Groups to exchange experiences, ask questions, and learn from community members who are already working in the SCM domain, get support you, answer questions, and share practical insights from their experiences. 
      For this, I recommend joining the Supply Chain Management Learning Group, where you can connect with fellow learners and professionals, exchange experiences, ask questions, and receive support from a community that shares your interest in SAP Supply Chain Management.

  5. Consider certification later

    • Once you have completed the foundational learning content and gained some practical understanding, you can evaluate whether an SAP certification aligns with your career goals. 
Of course, these are only recommendations. The best learning path depends on your individual career goals, your current role, the industry you work in, and the direction you would like to take in your SAP career.

Best of luck with your learning journey!
Kind regards
Margit

Thanks

Naveen

entry.title: Export SAP Integration Suite Inventory to Excel: Two Python Scripts for CPI iFlows and API Proxies.
entry.content:
If you have ever been asked how many iFlows sit on a tenant, or which API proxies belong to which product, you already know how it goes. You open the UI, click into one package at a time, and copy names into a spreadsheet by hand. It takes a while, and the list is out of date the moment someone adds a new flow. I got tired of doing this, so I wrote a couple of small scripts to do it for me.

There are two scripts. Each one reads a different part of SAP Integration Suite and writes the result straight into an Excel file. They use the standard public OData APIs with an OAuth client credential, so nothing is installed on the tenant. You run them from your own machine and you get a clean spreadsheet at the end.

Here is the one thing to keep straight while you read. There are two separate scripts for two separate jobs:

  • cpi_inventory_export.py reads Cloud Integration and lists every integration package and the iFlows inside each one.
  • apim_inventory_export.py reads API Management and lists every API proxy and the product it belongs to.

They look alike and run the same way, but they connect to different services in BTP and need different roles. To avoid any mix up, I have written each one as its own part below. The download link for both is at the end.

What each script produces

Script Reads from Output file Sheets you get

cpi_inventory_export.pyCloud IntegrationCPI_Inventory.xlsxPackages, iFlows
apim_inventory_export.pyAPI ManagementAPIM_Inventory.xlsxAPI Proxies, Products

The output is plain and easy to filter. The CPI file gives you one row per iFlow with its package name, so you can sort or pivot however you like. The APIM file gives you one row per proxy with the product it sits in, which is the view most people are really after.

Before you start

A few things need to be in place. These are the same for both scripts.

  • You can log in to the SAP BTP cockpit for your subaccount.
  • Cloud Integration or API Management is active in your Integration Suite tenant, depending on which script you want to run.
  • Python 3 is installed on your machine. You can check by opening a Command Prompt and running the command below.
python --version

If you see a version number come back, you are good. If the command is not recognised, install Python from python.org and tick the option to add it to PATH during setup.

Both scripts also need two small Python libraries. You only install these once, and the same two work for both scripts.

python -m pip install requests openpyxl

Part 1: Cloud Integration packages and iFlows (cpi_inventory_export.py)

This is the script that answers the question, what packages and iFlows do we have. It talks to the Process Integration Runtime service.

Step 1: Create the service instance

In your subaccount, open Service Marketplace and search for Process Integration Runtime. Open the tile and choose Create. On the Basic Info screen set these values:

  • Plan: api. This one matters. The integration-flow plan is only for sending messages to a deployed iFlow, and it will not list your packages. If you pick the wrong plan you get a 404 later.
  • Runtime Environment: Cloud Foundry
  • Space: your space, for example dev
  • Instance Name: cpi-extract

Step 2: Add the role

Move to the Parameters step. In the Roles field select WorkspacePackagesRead and keep the grant type as Client Credentials. This role is what lets the script read your packages and iFlows. If you leave it out, the API later tells you it cannot find the entity set, which is a confusing error for what is really just a missing role. Choose Create.

Step 3: Create the service key

Open the cpi-extract instance, go to Service Keys, and create one. Name it something like cpi-extract-key and leave the parameters box as the empty braces. Open the key in JSON view and copy these four values:

  • url
  • tokenurl
  • clientid
  • clientsecret

The Cloud Integration url usually has it-cpi in the host name and often ends with -rt. Make a mental note of that, because it is the easiest way to tell it apart from the API Management url in Part 2.

Step 4: Fill in the script and run it

Open cpi_inventory_export.py in any editor. Near the top, paste your four values inside the quotes and save. Keep the quotes in place.

CLIENT_ID = "your clientid"
CLIENT_SECRET = "your clientsecret"
TOKEN_URL = "your tokenurl"
API_URL = "your url"

Open a Command Prompt, move into the folder that holds the script, and run it.

cd "C:\path\to\your\folder"
python cpi_inventory_export.py

When it finishes you will see a line like Done. 12 packages exported to CPI_Inventory.xlsx. Open the file and you will find a Packages sheet and an iFlows sheet, sitting in the same folder as the script.

Part 2: API Management proxies and products (apim_inventory_export.py)

This is the script that answers the question, what API proxies do we have and which product is each one in. It talks to the API Management, API portal service, which is a different service from Part 1.

Step 1: Create the service instance

In your subaccount, open Service Marketplace and search for API Management, API portal. Take care to pick this tile and not the developer portal tile next to it. Open it and choose Create. On the Basic Info screen set these values:

  • Plan: apiportal-apiaccess
  • Runtime Environment: Cloud Foundry
  • Space: your space, for example dev
  • Instance Name: api-extract

 

Step 2: Add the role

Move to the Parameters step. Here the role is entered as JSON, not picked from a list. This is the step people most often miss, and skipping it is what leaves you with an empty service key later. Type the role, then choose Create.

{ "role": "APIPortal.Administrator" }

Do not have the Administrator role? You do not need it for this. The Administrator role allows create, update, and delete, but this script only reads. If you cannot or should not use Administrator, use the read-only role instead:

{ "role": "APIPortal.Guest" }

APIPortal.Guest gives read-only access to the API portal, which is all the script needs to list your proxies and products. It is also the safer choice if you are following least privilege, so if you are unsure, start with Guest. Whichever role you choose, it goes here on the Parameters step of the instance, not on the service key.

Step 3: Create the service key

Open the api-extract instance, go to Service Keys, and create one. Name it something like api-extract-key and leave the parameters box as the empty braces. Open the key in JSON view and copy these four values:

  • url
  • tokenUrl
  • clientId
  • clientSecret

The API Management url has the word apiportal in the host name. This is the biggest difference from Part 1. If you accidentally use the Cloud Integration host, the one with it-cpi, you will get a 404, so double check you copied the url from this key and not the CPI one.

Step 4: Fill in the script and run it

Open apim_inventory_export.py in any editor. Near the top, paste your four values inside the quotes and save. Keep the quotes in place.

CLIENT_ID = "your clientId"
CLIENT_SECRET = "your clientSecret"
TOKEN_URL = "your tokenUrl"
API_URL = "your url"

Open a Command Prompt, move into the folder that holds the script, and run it.

cd "C:\path\to\your\folder"
python apim_inventory_export.py

When it finishes you will see a line like Done. 1 proxies and 1 products exported to APIM_Inventory.xlsx. Open the file and you will find an API Proxies sheet and a Products sheet.

Quick comparison of the two scripts

If you take away one thing from this blog, take away this table. It shows exactly where the two scripts differ, so you always know which value belongs to which script.

  cpi_inventory_export.py apim_inventory_export.py

Service in BTPProcess Integration RuntimeAPI Management, API portal
Planapiapiportal-apiaccess
RoleWorkspacePackagesReadAPIPortal.Administrator, or APIPortal.Guest for read-only
url containsit-cpiapiportal
Output fileCPI_Inventory.xlsxAPIM_Inventory.xlsx

Common issues

These are the problems you are most likely to hit, with the quick fix for each.

  • Service key is empty. The role was not set on the instance. Recreate the instance with the role, then make a new key. For API Management, remember you can use APIPortal.Guest if Administrator is not available.
  • 404 not found. Wrong host or wrong plan. Use the url from the correct service key, and check the plan.
  • Entity set not found on CPI. Add WorkspacePackagesRead and create a new key.
  • 401 unauthorized. One of the four values is mistyped. Copy them again from the key.
  • No module named requests. Run the pip install command again.
  • SyntaxError when you run the command. You are inside the Python shell, shown by three greater than signs. Type exit() first, then run the command from the normal prompt.

Download the scripts

Both scripts are on GitHub with a short README. Download them here and follow along.

Download: cpi_inventory_export.py

Download: apim_inventory_export.py

If this saved you some clicking, drop a comment and tell me what you would like to see next. I am happy to extend the scripts, for example to add deployment status or the last changed date for each artifact..

entry.title: Re: Route Determination for One-Time Customers in S/4HANA...
entry.content: Hi Dominik, Thanks for your reply. Yes, we are receiving input file with one time BP address details but not Transportation zone. List of transportation zone is set up in supplier system so customer cannot send TZ in the inbound file. We are using extensibility to use postal code and add transportation zone in address detail and create sales order. Still, route is not determining in order but after creation, manually updating ship to is triggering auto route determination. I understood from SAP community portal that One time customer does not hold master data in the system so in initial EDI run, system won't go for auto route determination even TZ is updated in the address details. Please let me know if you are aware about any BADI to validate Order details before saving which can trigger route determination. Thanks for your help.

entry.title: SAP Note Creation Process for Customer Incident
entry.content:
Introduction :-
When customer raises a production-related incident in the ServiceNow tool, they provide issue details, scenario snapshots, and system information to help debug the reported issue. Based on this input after resolving the issue and testing in Internal systems. I followed a defined process to create the corresponding SAP Note and release note either tailored to the specific customer or applicable to all customers. 

 The process covers:

  • Creating the Request and associated Task 
  • Releasing the Transport Request (TR) 
  • Adding Corrective Instructions (Support Pack references) 
  • Creating and structuring the SAP Note 
  • Pilot Release and General Release process  

What is I7P?

I7P is SAP’s internal support and knowledge management system that serves as the central repository for SAP Notes. It is used by SAP teams to create, maintain, and track solutions for product issues before they are published to customer-facing support channels. As the backend source of SAP Notes, I7P helps ensure accurate technical knowledge, fixes, and support documentation are delivered to customers and also I7P system help to create Transport Request , Notes and to add support Pack based on customer system version. 

Transport Request and Task

Transport Request is the main container that holds all development changes. It is created whenever changes are made to repository objects such as programs, classes, and tables.

Task is a child object under a Transport Request. 

  • Must be released first before releasing the parent request.  
  • Tasks cannot be transported independently. 

Steps to Create ABAP Corrective Measures

Step 1: Open ABAP Corr. Measures

Log in to the I7P system and click ABAP Corr. Measures.

Step 2: Provide the Required Details

Fill in the required fields — Application Component, Short Text, Priority, and Software Component — and select the SAP Note radio button.

Step 3: Select Components and Systems

Select the components and systems where the changes are needed, then click Continue with System Selection.

Step 4: Choose the Transport Request Type

Select the type of Transport Request required based on the object changes — Workbench or Customizing.

The correction request is then created, as shown below:

Note: All Transport Requests and their associated tasks can be reviewed in transactions SE09 and SE10. Once all changes have been locked in the Transport Request, open transaction SE09, enter the user name, and click Display. Select the task and run a Consistency Check. If any errors are found, the task cannot be released until they are resolved. After a successful consistency check, click the Transport icon, as shown below.

Step 5: Retrofitting

Retrofit is a synchronization process used to copy changes made in a production or maintenance system into a parallel project or development system.

Go to transaction SCWB, enter the user ID (the request should be under the name of the person performing the retrofit), and click Execute.

Double-click the request whose changes need to be distributed to other systems, then select the request and click the Distribute icon, as shown below. All object changes are transported to the respective systems, and the system releases the tasks.

The system displays a success message confirming that the changes were distributed successfully.

Step 6 : Test Transport

Test Transport is the process of importing development changes from the DEV system into QAS for testing and validation prior to production deployment.

Right-click the request for the relevant system, select Process Item, then Generate Test Transport.

Press Enter, and a success message will appear.

 

Step 7 : Creating Correction Instructions and Adding the Support Package

A pop-up screen will appear — click Enter.

The correction request is created as shown below. Repeat this step for all the systems.

Double-click the created correction instruction to review the changes made to the object, add the dependent SAP Note, and add the Support Package.

Double-click the object name to view the code changes (inserted and deleted blocks).

Click Validity to add the Support Package (SP) — select n+1, where n represents 12 months

Select the block and press Enter.

Check and Release the Correction Instruction. 

Note: Correction instructions must be created for all the systems.

Step 8 : Creating the SAP Note

Double-click the created SAP Note and provide the symptom, solution, and objects changed for the issue. Add attachments if needed, and mention the relevant components in the SAP Note header.

Pilot Release – A customer number is added to the SAP Note so that the changes apply only to the designated customer and do not impact others.

General Release – The changes implemented through the SAP Note become applicable to all customers.

Step 9 : Final Step

After completing all development activities, SAP Note creation, correction instruction creation, and testing across various scenarios, the changes are thoroughly validated. Once all validations are successful, the SAP Note and correction request are assigned to the senior consultants for final review and release.

Conclusion 

By reading this blog,one will have a knowledge on Transport Request, Task, Retrofitting, Transporting changes from Development system to Quality system, Steps to create SAP Note, Release note.
Once the release note is issued, the customer can apply the note via SNOTE transaction and the reported issue will be resolved.

Thank you for reading!!

entry.title: Job Classification (EC) vs. Job Role (JPB): Two Objects You Have to Keep in Sync Yourself
entry.content:
Introduction

A question that comes up in almost every design workshop where Employee Central (EC) and Job Profile Builder (JPB) are both in scope: we already have Job Classifications in EC, so why do we need Job Roles in JPB? They sound interchangeable, and teams often assume maintaining one takes care of the other. It doesn't. Here's what each object is for, how they connect, and why a simplified 1:1 job architecture still means double maintenance.

The two objects and what they do

Job Classification is a Foundation Object in Employee Central. It's the administrative side of the job - assigned to positions and to Job Information, carrying attributes like pay grade, employee class and standard hours that drive business rules, reporting and integrations.

Job Role belongs to Job Profile Builder (Families and Roles). It's the talent side. It holds the job profile content: description, responsibilities, competencies and skills. This is what Recruiting, Performance, Succession and Career Development actually consume.

How they connect

The two are linked through a mapping maintained in Manage Job Profile Content, or via the Role–Job Code import template for bulk changes. The chain is simple: an employee holds a Job Classification in Job Information, the classification is mapped to a Role, and through the Role the employee gets their job profile.

Why the simplified design doesn't remove the effort

Many of us choose a simplified architecture: one Job Classification = one Job Role, same name, same code. Clean and easy to explain. But there is no automatic sync between the two objects. Creating a Job Classification does not create a Job Role, and renaming one does not rename the other.

Every job catalog change is therefore a three-step exercise: update the Job Classification in EC, update the Job Role in JPB, and maintain the Role-to-Classification mapping. Miss a step and nothing errors out - things just quietly break. An employee with no job profile, competencies missing from a performance form, a requisition pulling stale content.

What has worked for me: one owner and one intake process for catalog changes covering both objects, aligned external codes from day one, imports for mass changes, and a periodic reconciliation of active classifications against mapped roles.

Conclusion

Job Classification and Job Role answer two different questions: "what is this person administratively assigned to?" vs. "what does this job require?" SuccessFactors keeps them as independent objects with only a manually maintained mapping between them. A 1:1 simplified architecture is still a sound choice; it just doesn't eliminate the dual maintenance, so plan your governance around it rather than discovering the drift later.

entry.title: Getting Started with the New Git Access Feature in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
SAP Integration Suite recently added a built-in Git Access capability to Cloud Integration, letting you connect a GitHub repository directly to your tenant and push or pull integration artifacts natively from the Design workspace — no external CI/CD tooling or on-premise Git agent needed. In this post, I'll walk through the complete round-trip end to end: generating a GitHub token, connecting the repository, pushing an iFlow, merging it through a pull request, and importing it back into a new package via Git Import. Along the way I'll also flag a permissions error I ran into (GIT_OPERATION_FORBIDDEN) and how to avoid it, so you can get this working on the first try.

 

Part 1 — Preparation in GitHub

Step 1: Create a Fine-Grained Personal Access Token (PAT)

SAP CI authenticates to GitHub using a Personal Access Token, so the first stop is GitHub itself.

 

  1. Go to GitHub → Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens.
  2. Click Generate new token.
  3. Under Repository access, choose Only select repositories and pick the repository you want to connect (in my case, cicd-intsuite-builtin).

 

⚠️Tip / lesson learned: When I first generated a token without explicitly granting Contents (Read and write) permission on the repository, SAP CI was able to connect to the repo (fetching it for the picklist worked fine), but as soon as I tried to push an artifact I got:

Error in fetching branches from the given repository.

GIT_OPERATION_FORBIDDEN

Make sure you explicitly add repository permissions for at least:

  • Contents: Read and write
  • Metadata: Read-only (added automatically)
  • Pull requests: Read and write

 

Without the write permission on Contents, any push attempt from SAP CI will fail with the error above, even though the repository shows up correctly during setup.

  1. Generate the token and copy it immediately — GitHub only shows it once.

 

Step 2: Note the Repository Clone URL

You'll need the HTTPS clone URL for the repo, e.g.:

https://github.com/<your-username>/<your-repo>.git

That's everything needed on the GitHub side for now — the rest of the initial setup happens in SAP CI.

 

Part 2 — Connecting the Repository in SAP Cloud Integration

Step 3: Open Git Access Settings

In your Integration Suite tenant, navigate to:

Settings → Integrations → Git Access

You'll see a Repository Details table (empty the first time) with an Add button.

 

Step 4: Add the Repository

Click Add to open the Add Repository dialog and fill in:

 

  • URL: your repo's clone URL (e.g. https://github.com/<your-username>/cicd-intsuite-builtin.git)
  • Personal Access Token (PAT): the fine-grained token generated in Step 1
  • Name: a friendly name (e.g. cicd-intsuite-builtin)
  • Description: optional, but useful for documenting the repo's purpose

Click Connect to validate the credentials, then Add, and finally Save on the Git Access screen. You should see a confirmation toast: "Repositories saved." The repository now appears in the Repository Details table with its URL, description, and the user who added it.

 

Part 3 — Pushing an iFlow from SAP CI to GitHub

Step 5: Push an Artifact

Open an integration package containing an iFlow you'd like to version-control (e.g. Test Package for GIT Access), go to the Artifacts tab, and use the artifact's Push action.

In the Push dialog you'll select:

  • The Personal Access Token (pre-filled once configured)
  • The Repository
  • The Repository Branch — you can push to an existing branch or type a new branch name (e.g. dev) to create one on the fly
  • A Commit Message

If your PAT permissions are correctly scoped (see the warning in Step 1), the push succeeds and the artifact status updates to show something like:

Created | Branch: dev | Repository: cicd-intsuite-builtin

 

Step 6: Verify on GitHub

Switch over to GitHub and confirm the new branch was created with your commit — you'll typically see a banner like "dev had recent pushes X seconds ago" along with a Compare & pull request button.

 

Part 4 — Merging the Change in GitHub

Step 7: Open a Pull Request

Click Compare & pull request. Confirm the base branch (main) and compare branch (dev), add a title and description, and click Create pull request.

 

 

Step 8: Merge the Pull Request

Since there are no conflicts, GitHub will show "No conflicts with base branch — Merging can be performed automatically." Click Merge pull request, confirm the merge commit message, and click Confirm merge. Optionally delete the now-merged dev branch.

 

 

At this point, your iFlow — pushed from SAP CI — is committed on main in GitHub, complete with the full folder structure (META-INF, src/main/resources/scenarioflows/integrationflow, .project, metainfo.prop, and the .iflw file itself).

 

Part 5 — Importing an iFlow Back into SAP CI via Git Import

This is where the round-trip closes: you can pull artifacts from GitHub into a (new or existing) package.

Step 9: Add Integration Flow via Git Import

Create or open a package (e.g. Test Package for Import using GIT Access), go to Artifacts → Add → Integration Flow. In the dialog, three source options appear: Create, Upload, and Git Import. Select Git Import and provide:

 

  • Personal Access Token
  • Repository Name (chosen from the ones you've connected)
  • Repository URL (auto-filled)
  • Branch (e.g. main)
  • Integration Flow — a dropdown populated with the iFlows found in that branch/repo (in this example, Test_IFlow_for_GIT_Access)

 

Step 10: Add and Verify

Click Add (or Add and Open in Editor to jump straight into design mode). The iFlow is imported into your package, fully editable, and ready to be worked on — completing a clean push → PR → merge → import cycle entirely through SAP CI's native Git Access, with GitHub as the source of truth.

 

Summary

Where.     What happens

GitHub

Generate fine-grained PAT with Contents: Read & write, note repo URL

SAP CI

Settings → Integrations → Git Access → Add repository (URL + PAT)

SAP CI

Push iFlow from package → creates/updates a branch in GitHub

GitHub

Review, open PR, merge into main

SAP CI

Add Integration Flow → Git Import → pick repo/branch/iFlow → Add

 

Conclusion

In this post, we covered how to set up and use SAP Cloud Integration's native Git Access feature end to end — from generating a properly-scoped fine-grained GitHub PAT, connecting a repository under Settings, pushing an iFlow to a branch, and merging it through a standard GitHub pull request, to pulling it back into a brand-new package with Git Import. Along the way, you also learned how to avoid the GIT_OPERATION_FORBIDDEN error by making sure your PAT has Contents and Pull request permissions — a small but easy-to-miss detail that can otherwise cost you a fair bit of troubleshooting time. With this native capability, teams can now manage version control for their integration artifacts using standard Git workflows, without standing up separate CI/CD infrastructure.

I hope this walkthrough saves you some trial and error. If you have questions, run into a different error message, or have tips of your own to add, please drop them in the comments below — I'd love to hear about your experience with this feature.

Have you tried this feature yet? Let me know in the comments if you've run into other edge cases worth documenting.

entry.title: Launching the APAC Edition of Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise - A 7 part Webinar Series
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the Asia Edition of the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series.

A structured, seven-session programme designed specifically for architects who need to understand, evaluate, and act on SAP's Autonomous Enterprise strategy.  SAP's unique position (50 years of industry knowledge, the Knowledge Graph, semantically rich business data, and built-in governance) provides the structural foundation that generic AI approaches cannot replicate. But understanding how to architect for this shift requires more than reading documentation.

This series gives you direct access to the people building the platform, the reference architectures guiding decisions, and the practical patterns you need to translate strategy into delivery.

The Full Programme: Seven Sessions, One Architecture Journey

Register now: Register for the Series

 

No. Date Title Speakers

124 August 2026Unpacking the Autonomous Enterprise: The What, Why, and How for ArchitectsSindhu Gangadharan
21 September 2026Architecting From Intent to Action: Joule Work and the Knowledge GraphRahul Lodhe, Sai Jaganath Remisetti
38 September 2026The Architect's Playbook for Building Your Own SAP AgentsJason Luo, Venkateshwaran Viswanathan, Gaurish Dessai
422 September 2026An Architect's Guide to AI Agent Lifecycle Management and the SAP Family of ModelsLaurenz Vogt, Mock Seng Chong
529 September 2026The AI North Star Architecture and AI Golden Path: From Reference to RealityPVN Pavankumar, Paul Craigie
66 October 2026Data Foundations for an Autonomous Enterprise: From Fragmented Systems to Trusted ContextNiclas Schautkotter, Alan Beven
713 October 2026The Role of Enterprise Architects in Driving the Autonomous EnterprisePaul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch, Johannes Euler, Alex Aitken

Session 1 | 24 August 2026

Unpacking the Autonomous Enterprise: The What, Why, and How for Architects
Speaker: Sindhu Gangadharan
The keynote session that sets the stage. Sindhu Gangadharan unpacks the vision, architecture, and practical implications of the Autonomous Enterprise, covering three context gaps (process, data, governance) that keep enterprise AI stuck and how SAP addresses each structurally. You will be introduced to the five building blocks: Joule and Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite, Industry AI, the SAP Business AI Platform, and agent-led transformation.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the three context gaps and how SAP addresses each structurally
  • Map the five building blocks to your current landscape
  • Recognise what changes in your architecture practice: new UX patterns, domain maps, cross-domain orchestration, and governance as a first-class principle
Register for the Series

 

 Session 2 | 1 September 2026

 

Architecting From Intent to Action: Joule Work and the Knowledge Graph
Speakers: Rahul Lodhe, Sai Jaganath Remisetti
The way people interact with enterprise systems is fundamentally changing. Instead of navigating menus, filling forms, and manually connecting processes, people will express intent, and the system will orchestrate the response. This session covers how Joule Work introduces a dynamic engagement layer that interprets intent, learns from the Knowledge Graph, surfaces contextual insights, automates routine work, and orchestrates AI agents. The Knowledge Graph provides the semantic backbone: entity understanding, master data relationships, and business context that ground every agent interaction in meaning rather than guesswork.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the architecture of Joule Work as the unified engagement layer
  • Learn how the Knowledge Graph provides semantic and knowledge context: the difference between data and meaning for AI reasoning
  • Identify what your systems need to expose (APIs, events, semantic metadata) for Joule Work to operate effectively
Register for Session 2

 Session 3 | 8 September 2026

The Architect's Playbook for Building Your Own SAP Agents
Speakers: Jason Luo, Venkateshwaran Viswanathan, Gaurish Dessai
The SAP Business AI Platform provides a unified environment for building AI agents grounded in business context, but where do you start? What decisions define whether your agent works in a demo or in production? This session is a hands-on walkthrough of building an agent from scratch using the SAP stack: from Joule Studio and the SAP Integration Suite to the AI Core runtime. You will see the full development lifecycle: defining agent capabilities, grounding them in business data and process context, testing behaviour, and preparing for governed deployment.
Key Takeaways:
  • Walk through the end-to-end agent development lifecycle on the SAP Business AI Platform
  • Understand decision points: when to use deterministic applications vs. agents vs. agentic workflows vs. Knowledge Graph queries
Register for the Series

 Session 4 | 22 September 2026

An Architect's Guide to AI Agent Lifecycle Management and the SAP Family of Models
Speakers: Laurenz Vogt, Mock Seng Chong
As AI agents proliferate across enterprise landscapes, organisations face a defining architectural challenge: how do you discover, govern, secure, and measure the business impact of hundreds of autonomous agents? This session explores SAP's end-to-end approach to AI agent lifecycle management through the SAP AI Agent Hub (built on LeanIX), covering discovery and registration through governance, observability, and decommissioning. It also takes a deeper look at the SAP Family of Models, including RPT 1.5 which delivers 3.5x higher prediction quality than generic LLMs because it is pre-trained with business context.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the full agent lifecycle (discover, register, govern, observe, decommission) and how it maps to existing EA governance frameworks
  • Learn how the SAP AI Agent Hub on LeanIX connects agents to business capabilities, value streams, and organisational context
  • Learn what makes SAP's purpose-built models structurally different from generic LLMs
  • Identify the governance operating model needed before scaling from tens to hundreds of agents
Register for the Series

Session 5 | 29 September 2026

 

The AI North Star Architecture and AI Golden Path: From Reference to Reality
Speakers: PVN Pavankumar, Paul Craigie
Every architect navigating the Autonomous Enterprise asks the same question: what does the target architecture actually look like, and how do I get there from where I am today? The AI-Native North Star Architecture defines how SAP envisions the convergence of AI, data, and applications across the full stack. The AI Golden Path complements this with a guided journey from idea to production-ready application, including detailed decision trees for agents vs. deterministic applications vs. Knowledge Graph queries vs. HANA Vector Engine vs. agentic workflows vs. code-based agents. This session brings both to life with live demonstrations and worked examples.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the AI-Native North Star Architecture as the target state
  • Use the AI Golden Path decision framework for consistent build-vs-buy and pattern-selection decisions
  • See live demonstrations of how reference patterns translate into working implementations
  • Learn how to use published assets from the SAP Architecture Center as alignment tools with stakeholders
Register for the Series

 Session 6 | 6 October 2026

Data Foundations for an Autonomous Enterprise: From Fragmented Systems to Trusted Context
Speakers: Niclas Schautkotter, Alan Beven
The difference between isolated AI pilots and sustainable AI at scale lies in the foundational data architecture. Without trusted, connected, contextual data, agents hallucinate, predictions fail, and governance becomes impossible. This session covers how SAP brings data and AI together with a deep dive into SAP Business Data Cloud, including zero-copy data extraction, delta share protocol, BW modernisation, and the evolving role of the Data Architect in an autonomous landscape.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand why Data Context is the foundational layer of the Context System
  • Learn the architecture of SAP Business Data Cloud and BDC Connect, including zero-copy patterns
  • Understand the evolving role of the Data Architect: from ETL designer to context curator for AI agents
Register for the Series

Session 7 | 13 October 2026

 

The Role of Enterprise Architects in Driving the Autonomous Enterprise
Speakers: Paul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch, Johannes Euler, Alex Aitken
The closing session that brings it all together. When AI agents execute processes, orchestrate across domains, and make decisions grounded in business context, the architect's role shifts from system design to shaping the operating model, governance boundaries, and transformation roadmap. This session explores what the Autonomous Enterprise means for Enterprise Architects: how decision-making changes, what new competencies become essential, and how EAs position themselves as strategic connectors between business ambition and AI-enabled execution. Includes a complete series wrap-up and clear next steps.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand how the EA role evolves: from system integrator to transformation orchestrator and governance steward
  • Learn new competency areas: agent lifecycle governance, context architecture design, cross-domain orchestration, AI-native operating models
  • Take away a consolidated view of the series: key patterns, architectural decisions, and reusable assets
  • Connect with a community of architects navigating the same transition
Register for the Series

 Who Should Attend

This series is designed for:
  • Enterprise Architects evaluating AI-native operating models and governance frameworks
  • Solution Architects designing agent-based solutions on the SAP stack
  • Data Architects building the contextual data foundations that AI agents depend on
  • Technology Leaders responsible for AI strategy and platform decisions across Asia-Pacific
Whether you are early in your AI journey or already building agents in production, each session provides actionable architectural guidance grounded in real platform capabilities.
 
All sessions run at 11:30 AM IST | 14:00 SGT | 16:00 AEST | 08:00 CEST, designed for Asia-Pacific time zones with accessibility for colleagues in Europe.
Registration is open now and is completely free for everyone!
 
Register for all sessions: url.sap/aewebinar-apac
 
Each session is independent, but the series is designed as a progressive architecture journey. Attending all seven sessions will give you the complete picture. Select the sessions relevant to your role, or join the full series to build a comprehensive understanding of what the Autonomous Enterprise means for your architecture practice.

entry.title: Creating a Custom Action to Export Selected Records to Excel Using Fiori Elements and CAP
entry.content:
Introduction

While working on a Fiori Elements application, I came across a requirement that initially seemed straightforward but wasn't available as a standard feature. The application already had the built-in Export to Excel option, but it always exported every row displayed in the table. The business users wanted something slightly different—they wanted to select only a few records and export just those.

My first thought was to implement a backend action that generated an Excel file and returned it to the UI. After exploring the available options, I realized that wasn't necessary. SAPUI5 already provides a Spreadsheet API that can generate Excel files directly in the browser, making the implementation much simpler while avoiding additional backend development.

In this blog, I'll walk through the approach I used to add a custom Export Selected button to a Fiori Elements List Report. By the end, you'll have a working example that exports only the selected rows and integrates naturally with the standard Fiori toolbar.

Step 1: Initialize the CAP Project

First, create a new directory for your project and initialize a standard SAP CAP application. Open your terminal and run:

mkdir employee
cd employee
cds init

Step 2: Define the Database Schema

Next, we will define the data model for our employees.

Create a file named schema.cds inside the db folder and add the following code:

// db/schema.cds
namespace company.hr;
 
entity Employees {
    key ID     : UUID;
    name       : String;
    department : String;
    role       : String;
    email      : String;
}

Step 3: Create the Service and UI Annotations

Now, expose the database entity as an OData service and add the basic Fiori UI annotations to define what columns will appear in our Fiori List Report table.

Create a file named cat-services.cds (typically in the srv folder) and add this code:

// srv/cat-services.cds
using { company.hr as db } from '../db/schema';
 
service EmployeeService {
    entity Employees as projection on db.Employees;
}
 
annotate EmployeeService.Employees with @(
    UI.LineItem : [
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Employee Name', Value : name },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Department', Value : department },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Role', Value : role },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Email', Value : email }
    ]
);

Step 4: Add Mock Data

To test our application, we need some dummy data. SAP CAP automatically loads CSV files that match the namespace and entity.

Create a folder named data inside your db folder, then create a file named company.hr-Employees.csv and paste the following:

ID;name;department;role;email
1;Alice Walker;Engineering;Backend Developer;alice@company.com
2;Bob Smith;Sales;Account Executive;bob@company.com
3;Charlie Davis;Engineering;Frontend Developer;charlie@company.com
4;Diana Prince;HR;HR Manager;diana@company.com

Step 5: Configure the Custom Action in the Page Map

Once your Fiori Elements application has been generated, the next step is to add a custom action to the table toolbar.

Follow these steps:

  1. Right-click on the webapp folder in your project.
  2. Select Show Page Map.
  3. In the Page Map, select the List Report page.
  4. Click the Edit (pencil) icon to modify the page.
  5. Under the Table / Table Toolbar section, click the + button next to Actions.
  6. Select Add Custom Action.

Configure the custom action with the following properties:

  • Label: Export Selected
  • Requires Selection: Enabled
  • Press Event: exportSelected

Step 6: Implement the Custom UI5 Export Logic

Now, we will write the logic that extracts the selected rows and converts them to an Excel file using the Fiori V4 API.

Open the newly generated controller extension file (ListReportExt.js) and replace its contents with the following:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/export/Spreadsheet",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], function (Spreadsheet, MessageToast) {
    "use strict";
 
    return {
        exportSelected: function (oEvent) {
            
            // 1. Access the Fiori V4 API
            var oExtensionAPI = this.editFlow ? this : this.getExtensionAPI();
            
            // 2. Get only the rows selected by the user
            var aSelection = oExtensionAPI.getSelectedContexts();
 
            if (!aSelection || aSelection.length === 0) {
                MessageToast.show("Please select at least one employee to export.");
                return;
            }
 
            // 3. Extract the raw JSON data from those selected rows
            var aData = aSelection.map(function (oContext) {
                return oContext.getObject();
            });
 
            // 4. Map the Excel columns to your CDS database fields
            var aColumns = [
                { label: 'Employee Name', property: 'name' },
                { label: 'Department', property: 'department' },
                { label: 'Role', property: 'role' },
                { label: 'Email', property: 'email' }
            ];
 
            // 5. Configure the Excel Workbook properties
            var oSettings = {
                workbook: { 
                    columns: aColumns,
                    context: {
                        sheetName: 'Selected Employees'
                    }
                },
                dataSource: aData,
                fileName: "Custom_Employee_Export.xlsx",
                worker: false 
            };
 
            // 6. Build and Download the Excel file
            var oSheet = new Spreadsheet(oSettings);
            
            oSheet.build().then(function() {
                MessageToast.show("Excel export successful!");
            }).catch(function(sMessage) {
                MessageToast.show("Export failed: " + sMessage);
            }).finally(function () {
                oSheet.destroy(); // Destroy object to prevent memory leaks in the browser
            });
        }
    };
});

Step 7: Run and Test the Application

With the code in place, start your server by running:

cds watch

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

After running the application, select one or more employee records from the table. You'll notice that the Export Selected button is enabled only when records are selected. Clicking the button downloads an Excel file containing only the selected employees instead of the entire table.

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

What I liked most about this approach is that it keeps the implementation lightweight. Since the Excel file is generated in the browser using the SAPUI5 Spreadsheet library, there was no need to create a custom CAP action or write backend logic just for exporting data.

One thing I found useful was enabling Requires Selection for the custom action. Without it, users could click the button even when no rows were selected, resulting in unnecessary validation. Letting Fiori handle the button state provides a much better user experience.

Although this example exports employee data, the same approach can be used in almost any Fiori Elements application, such as Sales Orders, Purchase Orders, Expense Reports, or Products. If you've ever wanted to provide users with more control over what they export, this is a simple customization that's worth adding to your applications.

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite: як з'єднати SAP із будь-якою системою
entry.content:
📅 21 липня 2026  |  🏷 Інтеграція / SAP BTP

Сучасний IT-ландшафт компанії - це десятки систем: SAP, CRM, e-commerce, банківські API, логістичні платформи, хмарні сервіси. Змусити їх «говорити» між собою — завдання, яке вирішує SAP Integration Suite.

Що таке SAP Integration Suite?

SAP Integration Suite - це integration-as-a-service платформа в рамках SAP BTP, яка надає інструменти для побудови, моніторингу та управління інтеграціями між будь-якими системами: SAP-до-SAP, SAP-до-non-SAP, хмара-до-on-premise, API-менеджмент.

Ключові компоненти

•       Cloud Integration (iFlow) - головний інструмент: дизайн інтеграційних потоків у графічному редакторі

•       API Management - публікація, захист та моніторинг API

•       Open Connectors - готові конектори до 170+ зовнішніх сервісів (Salesforce, ServiceNow, Workday та ін.)

•       Integration Advisor - AI-асистент для маппінгу даних між різними форматами

•       Event Mesh - обмін подіями в реальному часі між системами

Як це працює на практиці

Уявіть: клієнт оформлює замовлення у вашому інтернет-магазині (Shopify). Дані автоматично потрапляють у SAP SD (замовлення на продаж), система перевіряє наявність товару у EWM, резервує запас, надсилає підтвердження клієнту через сервіс email-розсилки (SendGrid) і одночасно створює завдання у службі доставки (Nova Poshta API). Все це — один інтеграційний потік у SAP Integration Suite.

💡 Важлива деталь: SAP Integration Suite замінює старіший продукт SAP Process Integration (PI/PO). Якщо ваша компанія досі використовує PI/PO — плануйте міграцію. SAP підтримує PI/PO, але нових функцій там більше не з'являється.

Преднастроєні інтеграції: прискорюємо старт

SAP пропонує тисячі готових інтеграційних пакетів (Integration Packages) для найпоширеніших сценаріїв: S/4HANA ↔ SuccessFactors, S/4HANA ↔ Ariba, S/4HANA ↔ Concur, SAP ↔ Microsoft 365 тощо. Замість розробки з нуля — налаштування готового шаблону, що скорочує час і знижує ризики.

Кому варто розглянути SAP Integration Suite?

•       Компаніям із SAP, які хочуть інтегруватися з не-SAP системами

•       Тим, хто мігрує з PI/PO на сучасну хмарну платформу

•       Командам, що будують мікросервісну архітектуру навколо SAP

•       Бізнесам, що планують API-монетизацію своїх сервісів

Інтеграція - це не «разовий проект», а постійна дисципліна. SAP Integration Suite дає командам інструмент, де всі інтеграції видимі, моніторяться та управляються з одного місця. Це кардинально змінює ситуацію порівняно з «павутиною» point-to-point з'єднань, де ніхто не знає, що з чим пов'язано.

entry.title: IFRS - Migration of Contracts, Link to asset master, valuation continuation in S4 Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi SAP experts!

I need to migrate lease contracts and associated assets for my client. From the existing documentation, I understand that these must be migrated individually and then linked again in Valuation Management. We have successfully completed the migration; the asset master records and asset values match in both systems.

However, the situation is a bit more complicated when it comes to the contracts.

Our goal is to link the contracts to the corresponding migrated assets and to continue the valuation with the correct values starting from the migration date. I suspect that we need to define the correct combination of the following parameters:

  1. Start of Consideration

- What date should be entered here? Start of contract?

 

  1. First Posting From

- Is this the first day after the migration date? The date from which postings begin in the new system?

- This field also exists in the contract master record. Do the two always have to match?

 

  1. Probable End

- Mandatory or optional?

 

  1. Valuation Behavior

- The options here are Relative Difference (2), New Start (3), Absolute Difference (4), and Reduction (5).

Only with New Start (3) I have the option to select an asset. However, if I want to reuse an existing migrated asset, I must select one of the other behaviors. These, in turn, do not allow me to select an asset. What should be done here?

 

  1. Asset

- As mentioned in point 4, I can only select the corresponding migrated asset when the valuation behavior is “New Start” (3). However, the help section states that a new asset will be used in that case. This appears to be a contradiction.

 

We’ve already tried and simulated a number of things ourselves, but so far none of the approaches has resulted in a clean continuation of the valuation. Is there a documented, standardized procedure anywhere for our requirement to continue the contracts with migrated assets after migration?

I’d appreciate any advice!

Best regards,

Rado
